1: Theme — Feeding on wind, multiplying lies, making covenant with Assyria while sending oil to Egypt — the double-tongued pursuit of incompatible alliances. John 14:6: 'I am the way, the truth, and the life.' The wind-chasing and lie-multiplying of Ephraim is the polar opposite of Christ who embodies truth and the single clear path. No competing covenants: one Lord, one faith, one baptism (Eph 4:5).
2: Theme — The LORD has a case against Judah; will punish Jacob according to his deeds — divine judgment extends even to the relatively faithful. Rom 3:19: 'Now we know that whatever the law says, it says to those who are under the law, so that every mouth may be stopped, and the whole world may be held accountable to God.' Hosea's judgment on Judah-as-well is Paul's argument: no exception. Christ is needed by all.
3: Type — In the womb he grasped his brother's heel; in his strength he contended with God — the Jacob narrative as Israel's founding character. Christ is the true Israel who does not grasp by conniving but receives by the Father's gift. Where Jacob's heel-grasping defined Israel's origin, Jesus is the one who 'did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped' (Phil 2:6) — the anti-Jacob who reverses the grasping posture.
4: Type — He wrestled with the angel, wept, and pleaded for the blessing — the night of struggle and weeping prayer for divine favor. Luke 22:44: 'being in agony he prayed more earnestly; and his sweat became like great drops of blood.' Gethsemane is Christ's Peniel — the night of agonizing prayer seeking the Father's will. Jacob prevailed and received a blessing; Christ's submission to the cup also brings the ultimate blessing — resurrection and all authority.
5: Revelation of God — 'Even the LORD God of hosts — the LORD is his name to be remembered' — the divine name as the object of eternal memory and honor. Phil 2:9-11: 'God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name, so that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow.' The name of YHWH-to-be-remembered is the name given to Jesus: the one name at which all creation bows.
6: Theme — 'Return to your God; maintain covenant loyalty and justice, and wait for your God always' — the triad of covenant renewal: return, maintain hesed, wait. Titus 2:13: 'waiting for our blessed hope, the appearing of the glory of our great God and Savior Jesus Christ.' The waiting that Hosea prescribes — directed toward YHWH — is the waiting the NT directs toward Christ's return.
7: Theme — Dishonest scales; loves to take advantage — the commercial exploitation that violates covenant justice. Jas 5:4: 'The wages you withheld from the workers who mowed your fields are crying out against you!' James continues the prophetic pattern: economic exploitation as covenant violation. Christ comes as the one who perfectly maintained justice and calls his followers to the same.
8: Direct — 'Surely I have become rich; I have gained wealth for myself… no guilt in me' — the self-satisfied declaration of one who has confused material prosperity with moral status. Rev 3:17: 'For you say, I am rich, I have prospered, and I need nothing, not realizing that you are wretched, pitiable, poor, blind, and naked.' John addresses Laodicea with Hosea's exact Ephraim-diagnostic. The self-declared innocence of the prosperous is the persistent pattern Christ exposes.
9: Direct — 'I will make you live in tents again, as in the days of the appointed festival' — YHWH will bring Israel back to tent-dwelling simplicity, echoing the Feast of Tabernacles (Sukkot). John 1:14: 'The Word became flesh and dwelt [eskēnōsen, tabernacled/pitched his tent] among us.' The tent-dwelling YHWH calls Israel to return to is realized in the Incarnation: God himself takes up tent-dwelling in human flesh. The appointed festival's symbolism is embodied in Christ.
10: Direct — 'I spoke through the prophets; I multiplied visions and gave parables through the prophets' — the mode of progressive revelation: many voices, many forms, over time. Heb 1:1-2: 'Long ago, at many times and in many ways, God spoke to our fathers by the prophets, but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son.' This verse is the OT statement of the premise that Hebrews declares fulfilled: the many-prophets stage is the preparatory mode; the Son is the final, complete speech of God.
11: Type — Gilgal's altars become heaps of stones — the place of sacrifice and covenant-renewal becoming a ruin. 1 Pet 2:4-5: Christ is the living stone, and those who come to him become living stones, 'a spiritual house, to offer spiritual sacrifices acceptable to God.' Against every altar becoming a heap of stones, the living-stone temple Christ builds does not crumble because its foundation is indestructible.
12: Type — Jacob fled to Aram and served for a wife — the patriarch's servitude to gain his bride. Eph 5:25-27: 'Husbands, love your wives, as Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her.' Christ served not for seven years but gave himself entirely to gain his bride. Phil 2:7: 'taking the form of a servant' — Jacob's servitude for Rachel is a shadow of Christ's servitude for the church.
13: Direct — 'Through a prophet the LORD brought Israel up from Egypt, and through a prophet he was kept safe' — Moses as the prophetic deliverer. Acts 3:22: 'Moses said, "The Lord God will raise up for you a prophet like me from your brothers."' Deut 18:15-18 is the text; Acts and John 6:14 identify Jesus as that prophet. The prophet-who-delivered-from-Egypt becomes the type of Christ who delivers from sin's bondage.
14: Type — 'Ephraim has provoked his LORD to the deepest anger; his bloodguilt will be left on him' — the guilt remaining as the consequence of persistent rebellion. Matt 27:25: 'His blood be on us and on our children!' — the crowd's self-imprecation at the crucifixion ironically enacts the bloodguilt pattern. But the irony is that Christ's blood, when taken upon oneself in faith rather than in rejection, does not condemn — it cleanses: 1 John 1:7, 'the blood of Jesus his Son cleanses us from all sin.'
